article.body_preview:
DHAKA, June 5 -- Cash-strapped Bangladesh shut its biggest power plant today because it was unable to afford the coal to fuel it, as a sweltering heatwave creates surging electricity demand.

The 1,320-megawatt government-run Payra Power Plant in southern Bangladesh had already slashed production last month due to fuel shortages, but it was forced to go a step further today, manager Shah Abdul Mawla told AFP.

llm_connections.evidence_quote: The Bangladeshi taka depreciated about 25 per cent against the US dollar last year, driving up the cost of fuel imports and power utilities.
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article explicitly links a nationwide depreciation of the Bangladeshi taka to higher fuel import costs, matching the feature's focus on exchange-rate changes affecting import costs.

verification.quote_best_match: The Bangladeshi taka depreciated about 25 per cent against the US dollar last year, driving up the cost of fuel imports and power utilities.
verification.judge_reasoning: The exact sentence appears in the article and uses causal language ('driving up') to link a ~25% taka depreciation to higher fuel import and power utility costs, and it refers to the national currency—matching the country-level scope.
